class_schedule.topic: The Language of Business: Accounting, Users, and GAAP
learning_objectives: Explain the role of financial accounting as the language of business and identify its primary users
Distinguish between financial accounting and managerial accounting
Describe the role of GAAP, FASB, SEC, and the IFRS convergence project
Explain the fundamental accounting assumptions: economic entity, monetary unit, going concern, periodicity
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 1 - Introduction to Financial Statements
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 1 - Financial Reporting and Accounting Standards
SEC, 'What We Do' overview (sec.gov/about/what-we-do)

lecture_notes.introduction: Accounting is the language of business. Every decision to invest, lend, buy, sell, hire, or acquire a company is informed by accounting numbers. When a venture capitalist evaluates a startup, she reads its financial statements. When a bank makes a loan, it analyzes the borrower's balance sheet. When a CEO decides whether to open a new plant, she uses accounting data to estimate costs and returns. The field is divided into two halves. Financial accounting produces standardized reports for external users — investors, creditors, regulators, customers, suppliers — who are outside the organization and cannot demand custom information. Managerial accounting, covered in BA103, produces reports for internal managers making operational decisions. This course is about financial accounting: how companies record transactions, prepare financial statements, and communicate economic reality to the outside world. Because external users cannot verify the information themselves, financial accounting is heavily regulated. In the United States, the rules are set by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and enforced by the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) for public companies. The resulting body of rules is called Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). The rest of the world largely uses International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS), set by the IASB, and the two frameworks have been converging for two decades. Understanding GAAP is not optional for managers; every significant business decision has accounting consequences, and ignorance of those consequences can destroy value.
key_concepts: Financial accounting vs managerial accounting: financial accounting produces standardized external reports (income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement) governed by GAAP; managerial accounting produces internal reports tailored to decision needs with no external format requirements

Primary users of financial statements: investors (equity holders and potential buyers), creditors (banks, bondholders, suppliers extending credit), regulators (SEC, tax authorities), customers and employees (for firm stability), analysts (who aggregate and interpret)

GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles): the body of standards governing US financial reporting; set by FASB; a mixture of specific rules and general principles; distinct from IFRS which is principles-based
FASB (Financial Accounting Standards Board): private organization that sets US GAAP; operates under oversight of the SEC and funded through fees on publicly traded companies

SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission): federal agency that enforces federal securities laws; requires public companies to file audited financial statements; has authority to prosecute accounting fraud

IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards): principles-based standards used by over 140 countries including the EU, UK, Canada, Australia, and Japan; set by the IASB; increasingly converging with US GAAP
Economic entity assumption: the activities of a business are separate from its owners; Apple is accounted for separately from Tim Cook's personal finances even though he is the CEO
Monetary unit assumption: only transactions that can be measured in monetary terms are recorded; an earthquake that damaged a factory is recorded; a loyal customer base is not
Going concern assumption: the firm will continue to operate indefinitely; accounting measures assume normal operations, not liquidation; if going concern is in doubt the auditor must flag it
Periodicity assumption: the life of the business can be divided into artificial time periods (quarters, years) for reporting; these reports are estimates because transactions span periods
lecture_notes.demonstration: Pull up Apple's 10-K filing from SEC EDGAR and walk through the first 15 pages: the business description, risk factors, and the auditor's opinion. Identify where GAAP shows up (everywhere), who the auditor is (Ernst & Young), what the auditor is attesting to (fair presentation in accordance with US GAAP), and what the auditor is NOT attesting to (that the business is well-managed, that the strategy is correct, that the CEO is telling the truth about non-GAAP metrics like iPhone unit sales which Apple stopped disclosing). Contrast the formal, conservative tone of the GAAP sections with the promotional tone of the non-GAAP sections.
setup_requirements: Access to SEC EDGAR (sec.gov/edgar) for real 10-K filings
FASB Accounting Standards Codification access through the library or student subscription

review.value: Financial accounting serves people outside the company who cannot demand custom data; managerial accounting serves people inside who can; this difference drives everything — format, timing, rules, audit requirements

For public companies, GAAP is the law; non-GAAP metrics (adjusted EBITDA, free cash flow) can supplement but not replace GAAP disclosures; the SEC fines companies that hide GAAP bad news behind non-GAAP good news

The four basic assumptions (entity, monetary, going concern, periodicity) are easy to overlook but shape what can and cannot be in the financial statements; when a going-concern doubt arises, it is a material event that must be disclosed

GAAP requires judgment — on revenue recognition timing, asset impairment, reserve amounts — and those judgments shape reported results; 'creative accounting' lives in the judgment zones, not in the clearly-rules-based areas

class_schedule.topic: The Accounting Equation, Double-Entry, and the Balance Sheet
learning_objectives: State the accounting equation (Assets = Liabilities + Equity) and explain why it must always balance
Apply double-entry bookkeeping to record typical business transactions
Identify the major categories on a classified balance sheet
Distinguish between current and non-current assets and liabilities
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 2 - A Further Look at Financial Statements
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 5 - Balance Sheet and Statement of Cash Flows
Graham, The Intelligent Investor (rev. ed., 2006), Ch. 11 - Security Analysis for the Lay Investor

lecture_notes.introduction: Every accounting system in the world rests on one equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. This equation is not a convention; it is a tautology. Assets are economic resources the firm controls. Liabilities and Equity are the claims against those resources — liabilities are the claims of creditors, equity is the claim of owners. What you own equals what you owe plus what is left for you. If the equation does not balance, an entry is missing. Double-entry bookkeeping, invented by Venetian merchants in the 14th century and codified by Luca Pacioli in 1494, enforces this equality. Every transaction affects at least two accounts, and total debits always equal total credits. When you borrow $1,000 from a bank, you increase cash (an asset, debit) and increase a note payable (a liability, credit). When you pay $500 to a supplier for inventory, you increase inventory (asset, debit) and decrease cash (asset, credit). Every transaction keeps the equation balanced, and over five hundred years of commerce this system has proven itself the most reliable way to track economic activity. The balance sheet is the snapshot of the accounting equation at a point in time. It tells readers what the firm owns, what it owes, and what its owners have invested. Reading a balance sheet is one of the two or three most fundamental skills in business, and you will use it for the rest of your career.
key_concepts: Accounting equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity; the foundation of double-entry bookkeeping and the basis of the balance sheet; must balance after every transaction

Assets: economic resources that the firm owns or controls and that will provide future economic benefits; categorized as current (cash, receivables, inventory, prepaid expenses — expected to convert to cash within one year) or non-current (PP&E, intangibles, long-term investments)

Liabilities: obligations to transfer economic resources in the future; categorized as current (accounts payable, short-term notes, accrued expenses, current portion of long-term debt — due within one year) or non-current (long-term debt, deferred tax liabilities, pension obligations)

Equity: the residual claim of owners after liabilities are satisfied; also called net assets or book value; for corporations it includes contributed capital (common stock, additional paid-in capital) and retained earnings (accumulated net income minus dividends)
Double-entry bookkeeping: every transaction has at least two effects that keep the accounting equation in balance; enforces arithmetic discipline and provides internal cross-checks

Debits and credits: debit increases assets and expenses, decreases liabilities and equity; credit increases liabilities and equity, decreases assets; conventions inherited from 14th-century Italian ledgers
Classified balance sheet: groups assets and liabilities into current and non-current to show liquidity and solvency at a glance; standard format under GAAP
Working capital: current assets minus current liabilities; a measure of short-term liquidity; positive working capital means the firm can meet its near-term obligations

Book value vs market value: the balance sheet records assets at historical cost (for most items) which may differ dramatically from current market value; Apple's book value is a tiny fraction of its market capitalization because its intangible value (brand, ecosystem, future cash flows) is not on the balance sheet

Limitations of the balance sheet: does not show market value, does not capture internally-developed intangibles (brand, people, R&D), does not reflect off-balance-sheet items like operating leases before ASC 842 or certain derivatives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Record a complete set of transactions for a fictional coffee shop starting up: (1) owner invests $50,000 cash — increase Cash $50,000, increase Common Stock $50,000; (2) business takes $30,000 bank loan — increase Cash $30,000, increase Note Payable $30,000; (3) buys espresso machine for $15,000 cash — increase Equipment $15,000, decrease Cash $15,000; (4) buys coffee beans for $2,000 on credit — increase Inventory $2,000, increase Accounts Payable $2,000; (5) prepays $6,000 for 6 months of rent — increase Prepaid Rent $6,000, decrease Cash $6,000. After each transaction write out the accounting equation and show it still balances. Starting balance sheet: Assets = $77,000; Liabilities = $32,000; Equity = $45,000. Discuss what the balance sheet reveals: the business has liquidity ($57,000 current assets vs $2,000 current liabilities), is moderately leveraged (debt is 42% of total financing), and has not yet generated any earnings.
setup_requirements: Sample classified balance sheet for comparison (Starbucks 10-K)

review.value: If it doesn't, you have a bookkeeping error; this is the single most powerful cross-check in accounting and the reason double-entry has survived 500 years of business
Mixing current and non-current items hides liquidity problems; a company with large long-term assets and large short-term liabilities can be solvent on paper but unable to meet tomorrow's payroll

Coke's brand is worth tens of billions but appears nowhere on its balance sheet; internally-developed intangibles are expensed, not capitalized; this is why book value is usually much less than market value for brand-driven businesses

review.key: Historical Cost Is the Rule, Fair Value the Exception
review.value: Most balance sheet items are recorded at what they originally cost, not what they are worth today; this produces reliable but stale numbers; certain financial instruments and a few other categories are marked to market under GAAP

class_schedule.topic: Recording Transactions: Journal Entries, Posting, and the Trial Balance
learning_objectives: Journalize business transactions using proper debit and credit format
Post journal entries to T-accounts and calculate account balances
Prepare an unadjusted trial balance and verify equality of debits and credits
Identify and correct common bookkeeping errors
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 3 - The Accounting Information System
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 3 - The Accounting Information System
Horngren's Accounting (12th ed.), Ch. 2 - Recording Business Transactions

lecture_notes.introduction: Recording transactions is the daily work of bookkeeping. In practice it is done by software — QuickBooks, NetSuite, SAP, Xero — but every bookkeeper and every accounting student must be able to do it by hand because the software is just a fast calculator. If you cannot debit and credit by hand you cannot read an accounting system, trace an error, or make a correcting entry. The process is mechanical and repetitive: observe a transaction, identify which accounts are affected, decide whether each is debited or credited, and by how much, then enter the result in the journal and post it to the ledger. The journal is the chronological record. The ledger is the organized record: one account for cash, one for accounts receivable, one for inventory, and so on. At the end of the period, you check your work by preparing a trial balance: a list of every account and its balance. A matching trial balance does not prove correctness (you could have debited the wrong account or gotten the amount wrong), but a non-matching trial balance always proves an error. Modern accounting software hides this cycle from users, but the cycle is still running underneath, and understanding it is what distinguishes an accountant from a data-entry clerk.
key_concepts: Journal entry: the chronological record of a transaction, showing the date, accounts debited, accounts credited, amounts, and a brief description; debits are listed first, credits indented, and totals must equal
T-account: a simplified representation of a ledger account shaped like the letter T, with debits on the left and credits on the right; used for analysis and teaching
General ledger: the collection of all accounts; each account shows beginning balance, activity, and ending balance; the formal accounting record
Chart of accounts: the numbered list of all accounts used by a business; typically 1xxx assets, 2xxx liabilities, 3xxx equity, 4xxx revenues, 5xxx expenses
Posting: the process of transferring journal entries to the individual ledger accounts; historically done by hand, now automatic in accounting software
Normal balance: the side (debit or credit) where an account normally has a balance; assets and expenses have normal debit balances; liabilities, equity, and revenues have normal credit balances
Trial balance: a list of all accounts with their balances at a point in time, used to verify equality of debits and credits before preparing financial statements

Sources of error: transposition (writing 981 instead of 918), slide (writing 98 instead of 980), omission (forgetting an entry), commission (right amount in wrong account), principle (misclassifying an asset as an expense)
Correcting entries: used to fix errors discovered after posting; reverse the wrong entry and post the correct one
Subsidiary ledgers: detailed records for accounts with many sub-items; accounts receivable subsidiary ledger shows every customer's balance; the general ledger shows only the total
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through a complete month of transactions for a fictional consulting firm: (1) Day 1 — owner invests $20,000 cash; (2) Day 3 — pays $2,400 for 12 months of insurance; (3) Day 5 — performs $5,000 of consulting services on account; (4) Day 10 — collects $3,000 from the client; (5) Day 15 — pays $1,500 office rent; (6) Day 20 — buys $800 of office supplies on credit; (7) Day 25 — pays $500 in employee wages; (8) Day 28 — owner withdraws $1,000 for personal use; (9) Day 30 — performs $2,000 of services for immediate cash. Record each in journal format, post to T-accounts, and prepare the unadjusted trial balance. Then deliberately introduce a transposition error and walk through how to find it using the 'divide by 9' rule: the difference between debits and credits will be divisible by 9 if the error is a transposition.
setup_requirements: QuickBooks Online student access for modern software comparison
Calculator and chart of accounts handout

review.value: The journal captures transactions as they happen; the ledger organizes them by account; always enter in the journal first, then post to the ledger, to preserve the chronological record for audit

review.key: Matching Trial Balance Is Necessary But Not Sufficient
review.value: A trial balance that balances only proves that debits equal credits, not that the entries were correct; you can still have a wrong account, wrong amount, or missing entry
Transposition errors produce differences divisible by 9; sliding a decimal point produces differences that are multiples of 10; knowing the signatures helps find errors fast
QuickBooks does the posting automatically, but it still uses debits and credits underneath; when QuickBooks produces a weird balance, only someone who understands the mechanics can trace the error

learning_objectives: Distinguish between cash-basis and accrual-basis accounting and explain why GAAP requires accrual
Apply the revenue recognition principle and the matching principle to typical transactions
Prepare adjusting entries for accruals (revenues earned, expenses incurred) and deferrals (unearned revenue, prepaid expenses)
Prepare an adjusted trial balance and explain its role in financial statement preparation
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 4 - Accrual Accounting Concepts
FASB Accounting Standards Codification, ASC 606 - Revenue from Contracts with Customers (summary)
Schilit & Perler, Financial Shenanigans (4th ed., 2018), Ch. 2 - Recording Revenue Too Soon

lecture_notes.introduction: Accrual accounting is the single most important concept in financial accounting and the source of most of its complexity. In a cash-basis system, you record revenue when cash comes in and expenses when cash goes out. This is simple and matches the checkbook, but it produces misleading results for businesses that sell on credit, pay expenses in advance, or use long-lived assets. A magazine publisher receives a year of subscription payments in January. On a cash basis, January looks wildly profitable and the rest of the year looks terrible, even though the publisher has not yet delivered eleven months of magazines. Accrual accounting fixes this by recognizing revenue when it is earned (when the magazine is delivered) rather than when cash is received. The corresponding rule for expenses is the matching principle: expenses should be recognized in the same period as the revenue they helped generate. The result is a financial statement that shows economic reality, not cash timing. The cost is that accrual accounting requires judgment and adjusting entries. At the end of every period, the bookkeeper must record events that have not yet been captured by routine transactions: revenue earned but not billed, expenses incurred but not paid, assets used up during the period, liabilities settled by the passage of time. These adjusting entries are where most financial reporting errors and many frauds occur. The 2001 Enron fraud, the 2002 WorldCom fraud, and dozens of smaller cases all hinged on incorrect accruals.
key_concepts: Cash basis: record revenue when cash received, expense when cash paid; simple but misleading for credit transactions, prepaid items, and long-lived assets; not GAAP-compliant for most businesses
Accrual basis: record revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of cash timing; required by GAAP; produces a more accurate picture of economic performance

Revenue recognition principle: revenue is recognized when performance obligations to the customer are satisfied; under ASC 606 this is a five-step model — identify the contract, identify performance obligations, determine transaction price, allocate price to obligations, recognize revenue as obligations are satisfied

Matching principle: expenses should be recognized in the same period as the revenues they helped generate; if you ship $1,000 of inventory in March, the cost of that inventory is a March expense regardless of when the inventory was bought or paid for

Accrued revenues: revenues earned but not yet billed or collected — for example, consulting services performed in December where the invoice goes out in January; adjusting entry debits receivable and credits revenue

Accrued expenses: expenses incurred but not yet paid — for example, employee wages earned in the last week of December but paid in early January; adjusting entry debits expense and credits a liability

Deferred (unearned) revenue: cash received before earning it — magazine subscriptions, software licenses paid annually, airline tickets sold in advance; recorded as a liability when received, then transferred to revenue as earned

Prepaid expenses: cash paid for benefits that will be received in future periods — insurance, rent paid in advance, office supplies; recorded as an asset when paid, then transferred to expense as used

Depreciation: the systematic allocation of the cost of a long-lived asset (building, equipment) over its useful life; adjusting entry debits Depreciation Expense and credits Accumulated Depreciation (a contra-asset)

Adjusted trial balance: prepared after adjusting entries are posted; used to prepare the income statement and balance sheet; every adjustment crosses one income statement account and one balance sheet account
lecture_notes.demonstration: Continue the consulting firm example and make end-of-month adjustments. (1) Insurance: the $2,400 paid on Day 3 covers 12 months; allocate 1/12 = $200 to January insurance expense — debit Insurance Expense $200, credit Prepaid Insurance $200. (2) Supplies: $800 purchased; $300 remain; the other $500 were used — debit Supplies Expense $500, credit Supplies $500. (3) Unbilled services: additional $1,200 of work performed at month-end not yet invoiced — debit Accounts Receivable $1,200, credit Service Revenue $1,200. (4) Wages: employees earned $300 in the last two days of the month but will be paid next week — debit Wages Expense $300, credit Wages Payable $300. (5) Depreciation: office equipment has 5-year life, straight-line; monthly depreciation is $15,000/60 = $250 — debit Depreciation Expense $250, credit Accumulated Depreciation $250. After posting all adjustments, prepare the adjusted trial balance and verify equality.
setup_requirements: Sample 10-K notes on revenue recognition (Microsoft or Salesforce)

review.key: Revenue Recognition Is Not About Cash
review.value: The timing of revenue depends on when the customer gets the value, not when money changes hands; SaaS companies with big annual prepayments show less revenue than you'd expect because they recognize it monthly

review.key: Matching Is the Reason for Most Adjustments
review.value: Prepaid expenses, depreciation, and accrued expenses all exist to match costs to the periods that benefited; without matching, income would be wildly volatile from period to period
Cash received but not yet earned is a liability because the company owes the customer something in the future; this counterintuitive classification trips up many beginners
Fraudulent accruals let management pull revenue forward, push expenses into the future, or both; SEC enforcement actions are heavily concentrated in revenue recognition and expense accrual cases

class_schedule.topic: Closing Entries and the Complete Accounting Cycle
learning_objectives: Prepare closing entries for revenue, expense, and dividend accounts
Explain the distinction between permanent (real) accounts and temporary (nominal) accounts
Complete the full accounting cycle from transaction to post-closing trial balance
Describe the purpose and use of a worksheet in the closing process
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 4 (closing entries section)
Horngren's Accounting (12th ed.), Ch. 4 - Completing the Accounting Cycle
Wild, Shaw & Chiappetta, Fundamental Accounting Principles (25th ed.), Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Every accounting period ends with a closing process. Revenue and expense accounts are zeroed out and their net effect (income or loss) is transferred to retained earnings. Dividend accounts are also zeroed out into retained earnings. The logic is that revenue and expense accounts measure activity during a period; they are started fresh at zero at the beginning of each period so that the next period's activity can be measured cleanly. Balance sheet accounts — assets, liabilities, equity — carry their balances forward because they represent resources and obligations that continue across periods. The closing process is the bridge between periods. Once it is done, the books are ready for the new period and the old period's results are locked in. Before the closing process, the accountant prepares the financial statements (income statement, retained earnings statement, balance sheet) from the adjusted trial balance. The closing entries then update the balance sheet to reflect the income earned during the period, connecting the two statements. This session walks through the full accounting cycle: analyze transactions → record in journal → post to ledger → prepare unadjusted trial balance → make adjusting entries → prepare adjusted trial balance → prepare financial statements → make closing entries → prepare post-closing trial balance. Modern accounting software automates most of this, but managers who cannot do it by hand cannot diagnose problems when the software gets things wrong.
key_concepts: Permanent (real) accounts: assets, liabilities, and equity (other than dividends); balances carry forward from one period to the next; represent the cumulative position of the firm
Temporary (nominal) accounts: revenues, expenses, and dividends; balances are zeroed out at the end of each period; represent activity during the period

Income Summary: a temporary account used only during the closing process to consolidate revenues and expenses before transferring net income to retained earnings; appears only in closing entries, never in the financial statements
Closing entry 1: close revenue accounts to Income Summary — debit each revenue account for its balance, credit Income Summary for the total
Closing entry 2: close expense accounts to Income Summary — debit Income Summary for the total, credit each expense account for its balance
Closing entry 3: close Income Summary to Retained Earnings — debit Income Summary for net income, credit Retained Earnings (reversed for net loss)
Closing entry 4: close Dividends to Retained Earnings — debit Retained Earnings, credit Dividends for the balance

Post-closing trial balance: prepared after closing entries are posted; contains only permanent accounts (all temporary accounts are now zero); the last check of the accounting cycle before starting the next period
Worksheet: a columnar tool used to organize the closing process; columns for unadjusted trial balance, adjustments, adjusted trial balance, income statement, balance sheet

The accounting cycle: the nine-step process from transaction analysis through post-closing trial balance; repeated every accounting period, typically monthly for internal purposes and quarterly/annually for external reporting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete the consulting firm example by performing the closing process. The adjusted trial balance shows: Service Revenue $7,200; Rent Expense $1,500; Wages Expense $800; Insurance Expense $200; Supplies Expense $500; Depreciation Expense $250; Dividends $1,000. Step 1: debit Service Revenue $7,200, credit Income Summary $7,200. Step 2: debit Income Summary $3,250, credit each expense for its balance. Step 3: Income Summary now has a credit balance of $3,950 (net income); debit Income Summary $3,950, credit Retained Earnings $3,950. Step 4: debit Retained Earnings $1,000, credit Dividends $1,000. After closing, Retained Earnings has a balance of $2,950 ($3,950 net income minus $1,000 dividends), all revenue and expense accounts are zero, and the post-closing trial balance contains only balance sheet accounts. Prepare the final balance sheet and verify that Assets = Liabilities + Equity.

review.key: Temporary vs Permanent Is the Key Distinction
review.value: Revenue, expense, and dividend accounts measure activity; they reset each period; asset, liability, and equity accounts measure position; they persist; all of closing entries flows from this distinction

Income Summary exists only during closing and never appears on a financial statement; it is a holding account that consolidates revenues and expenses before transferring net income to retained earnings

review.key: Closing Connects Income Statement and Balance Sheet
review.value: Net income flows through closing entries into retained earnings; this is how the income statement and balance sheet articulate; a student who understands closing entries understands the articulation of the two statements

review.key: Post-Closing Trial Balance Is the Starting Point
review.value: The post-closing trial balance is the opening trial balance for the next period; any error left in it persists into the future

class_schedule.topic: Merchandising Operations and the Income Statement
learning_objectives: Prepare a multi-step income statement for a merchandising business
Account for purchases and sales of inventory under the perpetual inventory system
Compute gross profit and analyze its relationship to net sales and cost of goods sold
Record sales discounts, sales returns and allowances, and freight charges
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 5 - Accounting for Merchandising Operations
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 4 - Income Statement and Related Information
Penman, Financial Statement Analysis and Security Valuation (5th ed.), Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: A merchandising business buys finished goods and resells them to customers without transformation — think Target, Costco, Best Buy, Home Depot. Merchandising is the bridge between manufacturers and consumers, and it is the largest sector of the US economy by employment. Accounting for merchandising adds one major complication over service businesses: inventory. When Target buys a shipment of televisions, those televisions sit on the balance sheet as inventory (an asset) until they are sold. When they are sold, the revenue goes on the income statement as Sales, and the cost of those specific televisions moves from inventory to Cost of Goods Sold (an expense). The difference — Sales minus COGS — is gross profit, the foundation of retail economics. Gross profit divided by sales is gross margin, and watching this number tells you whether the retailer is maintaining pricing power. When Walmart's gross margin compresses, Wall Street panics; when Costco's grows, the stock rises. The multi-step income statement separates gross profit from operating expenses to give readers a clearer picture of where profits come from. This session covers the mechanics: recording purchases, recording sales, handling returns and discounts, calculating COGS, and producing a multi-step income statement that meets GAAP requirements.
key_concepts: Merchandising company: buys finished goods and resells them; common examples are retailers (B2C) and wholesalers (B2B)

Perpetual inventory system: inventory records are updated continuously with each purchase and sale; requires real-time tracking but provides current inventory levels and COGS; standard in modern retail due to barcode scanners and point-of-sale software
Periodic inventory system: inventory records are updated only at the end of the period by physical count; simpler but loses visibility into inventory levels during the period
Purchase entry (perpetual): debit Inventory, credit Cash or Accounts Payable for the purchase amount; freight-in, if paid by buyer, is also added to Inventory
Sale entry (perpetual): two entries — (1) debit Cash or Accounts Receivable, credit Sales Revenue for the selling price; (2) debit Cost of Goods Sold, credit Inventory for the cost of the items sold
Sales returns and allowances: a contra-revenue account that tracks returns and price adjustments; the return also reverses the COGS entry to restore inventory
Sales discounts: a contra-revenue account tracking cash discounts offered for early payment (e.g., 2/10, n/30 means 2% discount if paid within 10 days, full amount due in 30 days)
Gross profit: Net Sales minus Cost of Goods Sold; the primary measure of merchandising efficiency

Multi-step income statement: Net Sales - COGS = Gross Profit; Gross Profit - Operating Expenses = Operating Income; Operating Income +/- Non-operating Items = Pretax Income; Pretax Income - Income Tax = Net Income

Operating vs non-operating items: operating items relate to the firm's core business (sales, cost of goods, selling and administrative expenses); non-operating items are incidental (interest income, interest expense, gains and losses on asset sales)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through a full set of merchandising transactions for a clothing retailer. (1) Buy 100 shirts at $20 each from supplier on credit, 2/10 n/30 — debit Inventory $2,000, credit Accounts Payable $2,000. (2) Pay freight of $50 — debit Inventory $50, credit Cash $50 (inventory cost now $20.50 per shirt). (3) Sell 60 shirts at $45 each for cash — entry 1: debit Cash $2,700, credit Sales Revenue $2,700; entry 2: debit COGS $1,230, credit Inventory $1,230. (4) Customer returns 3 shirts — debit Sales Returns $135, credit Cash $135; debit Inventory $61.50, credit COGS $61.50. (5) Pay supplier within discount window — debit Accounts Payable $2,000, credit Cash $1,960, credit Inventory $40. Prepare the multi-step income statement: Net Sales $2,565; COGS $1,168.50; Gross Profit $1,396.50; Gross Margin 54.4%. Apparel retailers typically run 40-55%.
setup_requirements: Industry gross margin benchmarks (Damodaran or investopedia)
Sample multi-step income statement (Target or Costco 10-K)

review.value: Under the perpetual system, every sale requires both a revenue entry and a COGS entry; forgetting the COGS entry is a common bookkeeping error and overstates gross profit
For a retailer, gross profit is the first and most important metric; if gross profit cannot cover operating expenses, the business cannot be profitable no matter how much it sells

Sales Returns and Sales Discounts are recorded separately rather than netting against Sales Revenue, so management can see return rates and discount usage over time; these are important signals of customer satisfaction and pricing pressure

The multi-step income statement separates operating from non-operating and isolates gross profit; this is what analysts need; the single-step format is simpler but hides the structure of profitability

class_schedule.topic: Inventory Costing Methods: FIFO, LIFO, and Weighted Average
learning_objectives: Apply the FIFO, LIFO, and weighted-average inventory costing methods to compute ending inventory and COGS
Analyze the effects of inventory costing methods on financial statements during periods of changing prices
Explain the lower of cost or net realizable value rule and apply it to inventory write-downs
Evaluate the financial statement and tax implications of the LIFO/FIFO choice
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 6 - Inventories
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 8 - Valuation of Inventories
FASB ASC 330 - Inventory

lecture_notes.introduction: Inventory costing is one of the few places in financial accounting where management gets to make a major choice that affects reported results. When a business buys inventory at different prices over time — which is almost always the case — it must decide which costs attach to which units when those units are sold. Three methods are allowed under US GAAP: First-In, First-Out (FIFO), Last-In, First-Out (LIFO), and Weighted Average Cost. Under IFRS, LIFO is not allowed, which is why US companies that use LIFO often disclose a 'LIFO reserve' so international analysts can convert their financials. During periods of rising prices (inflation), FIFO reports higher income and higher ending inventory (because the oldest, cheapest units are charged to COGS and the newest, most expensive remain in inventory). LIFO reports lower income and lower ending inventory. Weighted Average lands between. The choice has real cash consequences through taxes. Many US companies (particularly oil and gas, retailers) use LIFO specifically to reduce taxable income in inflationary times, even though it makes the balance sheet less informative. This is a rare case in accounting where tax planning drives financial reporting.
key_concepts: Specific identification: tracks the actual cost of each individual unit; used for unique, high-value items (automobiles, real estate, fine art); most accurate but impractical for fungible goods

First-In, First-Out (FIFO): assumes the oldest units are sold first; ending inventory consists of the newest (most recent) costs; during rising prices, produces higher net income and higher ending inventory

Last-In, First-Out (LIFO): assumes the newest units are sold first; ending inventory consists of the oldest costs; during rising prices, produces lower net income and lower ending inventory; not allowed under IFRS
Weighted Average Cost: computes a single average cost per unit across all purchases; each unit sold is charged the average; lands between FIFO and LIFO during price changes
Moving average: a weighted average recomputed after each purchase; used under the perpetual inventory system
LIFO reserve: the difference between LIFO inventory and what inventory would be under FIFO; disclosed by LIFO firms to allow restatement to FIFO for comparison
LIFO liquidation: when a LIFO firm sells more units than it buys in a period, the old low-cost layers are charged to COGS, producing a one-time spike in reported income; a warning sign
Consistency principle: once a firm chooses an inventory method, it must use the same method from period to period; changes require disclosure and a cumulative-effect adjustment

Lower of cost or net realizable value (LCNRV): when the net realizable value (selling price minus selling costs) falls below cost, inventory must be written down to NRV; an immediate expense; a permanent reduction
Inventory turnover: Cost of Goods Sold divided by average inventory; measures how many times per year inventory is sold and replaced; typical values range from 2 (luxury) to 30+ (grocery)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply all three methods to the same scenario. A hardware store has the following for a particular drill: Beginning inventory 10 units at $80 each ($800). March 5: buy 20 units at $85 ($1,700). March 15: sell 15 units. March 20: buy 15 units at $90 ($1,350). March 25: sell 20 units. Total units available: 45. Total units sold: 35. Ending inventory: 10 units. Total cost of units available: $3,850. FIFO: COGS charges oldest costs first. Sell 10 at $80 + 20 at $85 + 5 at $90 = $2,950 COGS. Ending inventory = 10 at $90 = $900. LIFO (periodic): COGS charges newest costs first. 15 at $90 + 20 at $85 = $3,050 COGS. Ending inventory = 10 at $80 = $800. Weighted Average: average cost = $3,850 / 45 = $85.56 per unit. COGS = 35 × $85.56 = $2,994.44. Ending inventory = 10 × $85.56 = $855.56. FIFO gives highest ending inventory and lowest COGS; LIFO gives lowest ending inventory and highest COGS. ExxonMobil uses LIFO and in 2022 disclosed a LIFO reserve of over $7 billion, representing decades of tax savings.

review.value: Inventory method affects COGS (income statement), ending inventory (balance sheet), taxes (cash flow), and every ratio involving inventory or income; the choice is not cosmetic
The main reason to choose LIFO in the US is to lower taxable income during inflation; the trade-off is a less informative balance sheet; IFRS countries don't have this option because they banned LIFO

If inventory's net realizable value drops below cost, it must be written down immediately; the write-down is an expense, not a gradual depreciation; old electronics inventory is written down every quarter at big retailers

Inventory turnover reveals operational efficiency; a retailer whose turnover is falling is accumulating obsolete stock that will eventually be written down; watch turnover trends, not just absolute levels

learning_objectives: Describe the components of the COSO internal control framework
Identify common types of fraud and the conditions that enable them (fraud triangle)
Prepare a bank reconciliation and record the adjusting entries that arise from it
Explain the role of Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX) in post-Enron financial reporting
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 7 - Fraud, Internal Control, and Cash
COSO, Internal Control — Integrated Framework (2013), Executive Summary
Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, Sections 302, 404, 906 (summaries)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every accounting system is also a control system. It records what happened, but it also establishes who authorized the transaction, who executed it, who verified it, and who reviewed the result. These controls exist because humans are fallible and some are dishonest. A business without internal controls will eventually lose money to error and theft, and the losses in large companies can be staggering. Enron was one of the largest bankruptcies in history, driven by accounting fraud that senior management designed specifically to evade internal controls. WorldCom followed soon after. Together, these cases triggered the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, which completely reshaped the internal control environment for public companies in the US. Sarbanes-Oxley made CEOs and CFOs personally certify the accuracy of financial statements (Section 302), required management to assess internal control effectiveness (Section 404), and imposed criminal penalties for fraudulent certification (Section 906). This session covers the internal control framework (COSO), the fraud triangle that explains why fraud happens, and the most common fraud type: cash theft or skimming. We close with bank reconciliation, the single most important cash control for small and medium businesses.
key_concepts: COSO Internal Control Framework: developed by the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations; identifies five components — control environment (tone at top), risk assessment, control activities, information and communication, monitoring
Control environment: the foundation; tone at the top, integrity, competence, organizational structure, board oversight; if the control environment is weak, detailed controls don't help
Control activities: specific policies and procedures — segregation of duties, authorization requirements, physical safeguards, independent verification, documentation

Segregation of duties: no single individual should handle a transaction end-to-end; custody of assets, authorization, and record-keeping should be separated among different people; the single most important control

Fraud triangle (Donald Cressey): fraud requires three conditions — pressure (financial need, unrealistic targets), opportunity (weak controls), and rationalization (the perpetrator tells themselves it is acceptable); removing any one prevents fraud
Common frauds: cash skimming, check tampering, fraudulent disbursements, payroll schemes (ghost employees), financial statement fraud

Sarbanes-Oxley Section 302: CEO and CFO must personally certify that quarterly and annual reports fairly present the company's condition; criminal penalties for false certification up to $5 million and 20 years in prison
Sarbanes-Oxley Section 404: management must assess and report on internal controls over financial reporting; external auditor must issue an opinion on management's assessment (for larger companies)
PCAOB (Public Company Accounting Oversight Board): created by Sarbanes-Oxley to oversee audits of public companies; ended the accounting profession's self-regulation

Bank reconciliation: the monthly process of comparing the company's cash balance per books with the cash balance per bank statement; identifies outstanding checks, deposits in transit, bank fees, NSF checks, and potential errors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the Enron fraud as a case study in control failure. Enron used special-purpose entities (SPEs) to hide billions in debt off its balance sheet while reporting booming earnings. The SPEs were controlled by Enron's own CFO, Andrew Fastow, who also received side payments from them. The fraud exploited weaknesses in several control components: (1) Control environment — a culture of pressure to hit numbers; (2) Segregation of duties — Fastow ran both the SPEs and Enron's finance function; (3) Board oversight — the audit committee lacked expertise; (4) External auditor — Arthur Andersen, conflicted by consulting fees. The outcome: bankruptcy, $60 billion in lost shareholder value, 20,000 employees lost their jobs, criminal convictions of CEO Jeff Skilling and Chairman Ken Lay, collapse of Arthur Andersen, and passage of Sarbanes-Oxley. Then walk through a bank reconciliation: identify outstanding checks ($1,200), deposits in transit ($800), bank service charges ($50), and an NSF check from a customer ($300).
setup_requirements: Enron case background (NYT or WSJ retrospectives)

review.key: Tone at the Top Beats Rules
review.value: No amount of procedural controls can compensate for a CEO who signals that hitting numbers is more important than following the rules; Enron's controls existed on paper but were systematically overridden

review.key: Segregation of Duties Is the Core
review.value: When one person can authorize, execute, and record a transaction, fraud becomes easy; separating these functions is inconvenient but essential, and its absence is the first thing auditors look for
Fraud is not random; it happens when pressure, opportunity, and rationalization align; removing opportunity (via controls) is the lever managers have the most control over
Monthly bank reconciliation is cheap, easy, and catches both honest errors and many fraud schemes; skipping it is one of the most common small-business control failures

class_schedule.topic: Receivables: Accounting for Credit Sales and Uncollectibles
learning_objectives: Record transactions involving accounts receivable including sales, collections, and write-offs
Apply the allowance method to estimate and record bad debt expense
Compare the percentage of receivables and percentage of sales approaches to estimating uncollectibles
Calculate and interpret the accounts receivable turnover ratio and average collection period
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 8 - Accounting for Receivables
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 7 - Cash and Receivables
FASB ASU 2016-13 - Credit Losses (CECL model summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: When a business sells on credit, it converts inventory into a receivable — a legal right to future cash. Receivables are common because credit sales drive volume; most B2B commerce in the US runs on net-30 terms. But receivables are also a source of loss. Not every customer pays. Some go bankrupt, some dispute the invoice, some simply never pay. Accounting for these potential losses is governed by the matching principle: the bad debt expense associated with this year's credit sales should be recognized this year, not when the specific customer is identified as uncollectible. This is the job of the allowance method. Under the allowance method, at the end of each period the company estimates the portion of receivables that will not be collected and records an allowance (a contra-asset) for that amount. When a specific account is later written off, the write-off reduces both the receivable and the allowance with no income statement effect — the expense was already recognized. The alternative, the direct write-off method, only recognizes bad debt when a specific account is identified as uncollectible; this violates matching and is only allowed for tax purposes or for immaterial amounts. This session also covers CECL (Current Expected Credit Losses), which since 2020 requires banks and certain other lenders to estimate lifetime expected losses on loans at origination — a response to the 2008 crisis, when banks waited too long to recognize losses.
key_concepts: Accounts receivable: amounts owed by customers for goods or services delivered on credit; the most common current asset after cash; typically measured net of an allowance for doubtful accounts
Credit sale entry: debit Accounts Receivable, credit Sales Revenue for the invoice amount; COGS and inventory entries as usual under perpetual system
Collection entry: debit Cash, credit Accounts Receivable for the amount received
Allowance method: estimates bad debts in advance and records them as an expense matched to the period of the sale; creates an Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (contra-asset)
Allowance entry: debit Bad Debt Expense, credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts for the estimated uncollectible amount
Write-off entry: debit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts, credit Accounts Receivable for the specific customer's balance; no income statement effect because the expense was recognized earlier
Percentage of sales method: estimates bad debt expense as a percentage of credit sales (e.g., 1.5% of credit sales); focuses on the income statement

Percentage of receivables method: estimates the required balance in the allowance as a percentage of receivables, often using an aging schedule with different rates for different age buckets; focuses on the balance sheet

Aging of receivables: categorizes receivables by how long they have been outstanding (current, 1-30 days, 31-60, 61-90, 90+); older receivables have higher default probability; gives a more accurate estimate than a single percentage

Accounts receivable turnover: Net Credit Sales divided by Average Accounts Receivable; measures how quickly receivables are collected; typical values range from 5 (long-cycle B2B) to 40+ (fast-turn retailers)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through a complete receivables cycle for a wholesaler. (1) Make $500,000 in credit sales — debit Accounts Receivable $500,000, credit Sales Revenue $500,000. (2) Collect $450,000 — debit Cash $450,000, credit Accounts Receivable $450,000. (3) Year-end aging: $25,000 current (0.5%); $10,000 in 1-30 (2%); $5,000 in 31-60 (5%); $3,000 in 61-90 (15%); $7,000 over 90 days (40%). Required allowance = $125 + $200 + $250 + $450 + $2,800 = $3,825. (4) Current allowance balance is $500; bad debt expense = $3,825 - $500 = $3,325. Entry: debit Bad Debt Expense $3,325, credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts $3,325. (5) Later, a customer with a $2,000 balance files bankruptcy — debit Allowance $2,000, credit Accounts Receivable $2,000. No income statement effect. (6) Receivables turnover: $500,000 / (($30,000 + $50,000)/2) = 12.5, consistent with 30-day terms.
setup_requirements: Sample allowance for doubtful accounts disclosures

review.key: Match the Expense to the Sale
review.value: The allowance method exists to match bad debt expense to the period of the sale; waiting for a specific write-off violates matching and produces erratic income
Old receivables are much less likely to be collected than recent ones; an aging schedule produces a far more accurate estimate than a single percentage of total receivables
When a receivable is written off, there is no income statement impact because the loss was already recognized via the earlier allowance entry; this surprises students but is logically consistent
A sudden drop in receivables turnover signals that collections are slowing, which often precedes credit losses or customer financial distress; analysts watch this ratio closely

class_schedule.topic: Long-Lived Assets: Acquisition, Depreciation, and Disposal
learning_objectives: Determine the acquisition cost of plant assets including capitalizable costs
Apply straight-line, units-of-production, and double-declining-balance depreciation methods
Account for asset disposals including sales at gain, loss, and salvage value
Distinguish between capital expenditures and revenue expenditures
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 9 - Plant Assets, Natural Resources, and Intangible Assets
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 10-11 - PP&E
Higgins, Analysis for Financial Management (13th ed.), Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Property, plant, and equipment (PP&E) — the factories, offices, trucks, machinery, and computers a business owns — are typically the largest assets on a non-financial company's balance sheet. Accounting for these long-lived assets raises three questions: what costs should be capitalized when the asset is acquired, how should the cost be spread over the asset's useful life (depreciation), and how should the disposal of the asset be recorded. When a trucking company buys a new tractor, the capitalized cost includes not just the invoice price but also freight, taxes, and any costs to get the tractor ready for use. It does not include the driver's first-week salary or fuel — those are operating expenses. When the tractor is depreciated, the cost is spread over its useful life using one of several methods. Straight-line is by far the most common. Accelerated methods like double-declining-balance charge more in early years. Units-of-production ties depreciation to actual usage. The method choice affects reported income, but not cash flow (depreciation is non-cash) or total lifetime expense.
key_concepts: Plant assets (PP&E): tangible assets used in operations with useful lives longer than one year; includes land, buildings, machinery, equipment, vehicles, furniture

Capitalizable costs: all expenditures necessary to acquire the asset and get it ready for its intended use; includes purchase price, freight, installation, testing, legal fees, sales tax; excludes operating costs after the asset is in use
Depreciation: the systematic allocation of the cost of a plant asset to expense over its useful life; a non-cash expense; a means of matching cost to benefit periods, not a valuation mechanism
Straight-line depreciation: (Cost - Salvage Value) / Useful Life; equal annual charges; simple and most common; appropriate when the asset is used roughly evenly over time
Units-of-production: (Cost - Salvage Value) / Total Expected Units × Units Produced This Period; depreciation tracks actual usage; appropriate for assets like mining equipment
Double-declining-balance: 2 × Straight-line Rate × Book Value at Beginning of Period; accelerated method that charges more in early years; ignores salvage value until book value approaches it
Useful life and salvage value: both are estimates, revised over time as information improves; changes in estimates are handled prospectively without restating prior periods

Capital expenditure vs revenue expenditure: capital expenditures extend useful life or increase productivity (new engine, major renovation) and are capitalized; revenue expenditures maintain current condition (oil changes, repairs) and are expensed immediately
Impairment: when an asset's carrying value exceeds its recoverable amount, it must be written down; the write-down is an immediate expense; reversals allowed under IFRS but not GAAP
Asset disposal: when sold, retired, or destroyed, the asset's book value is removed from the books; any difference between proceeds and book value is a gain or loss
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the full life of a piece of equipment. (1) Purchase: $100,000 invoice, freight $5,000, installation $8,000, test run raw material $2,000. Capitalized cost = $115,000. Useful life 5 years. Salvage $15,000. (2) Straight-line: ($115,000 - $15,000) / 5 = $20,000 per year. Book value end of year 1: $95,000. (3) Double-declining-balance: rate = 40%. Year 1: 40% × $115,000 = $46,000. Year 2: 40% × $69,000 = $27,600. Switch to straight-line in later years to avoid depreciating below salvage. (4) Sale at end of year 3 for $40,000 (straight-line): book value $55,000, loss $15,000. Entry: debit Cash $40,000, debit Accumulated Depreciation $60,000, debit Loss on Sale $15,000, credit Equipment $115,000. WorldCom's fraud (2002) was capitalizing $3.8 billion of operating costs as capital expenditures, inflating assets and income; CEO Bernie Ebbers got 25 years in prison.
setup_requirements: Sample PP&E disclosures from any manufacturer 10-K

review.key: Capitalize What's Necessary to Prepare the Asset
review.value: Freight, installation, and setup costs are part of the asset; operating costs after the asset is in use are not; this line generates audit disputes
Depreciation allocates cost to periods; it is not an attempt to match the asset's market value; an old, fully-depreciated machine may still be worth real money

review.key: Method Choice Affects Timing, Not Total
review.value: Over the life of an asset, total depreciation is the same under any method (cost minus salvage); different methods just rearrange the timing

review.key: Capital vs Revenue Is a Judgment Call
review.value: WorldCom's fraud was capitalizing operating costs as capital expenditures; the line is policed by auditors but judgment always exists and can be abused

class_schedule.topic: Liabilities: Current, Long-Term Debt, and Bonds
learning_objectives: Account for current liabilities including accounts payable, accrued expenses, and current portion of long-term debt
Describe the features of a bond and distinguish between par, discount, and premium bonds
Record the issuance, interest payments, and retirement of bonds using the effective interest method
Evaluate the debt-to-equity ratio and other solvency measures
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 10 - Liabilities
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 13-14 - Current and Long-Term Liabilities
Fabozzi, Bond Markets, Analysis, and Strategies (9th ed.), Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Most businesses borrow money. The cheapest source of financing is usually supplier credit (accounts payable) and the most expensive is usually equity. In between are bank loans, lines of credit, commercial paper, and bonds. Accounting for liabilities is generally more straightforward than accounting for assets because liabilities are contractual — the amount owed is written in the loan agreement or bond indenture — but there are still complications around timing, measurement, and classification. This session focuses on the three most common liability categories. Current liabilities include everything due within one year. Long-term debt includes bank loans, mortgages, and bonds with maturities beyond one year. Bonds get special attention because they are the dominant source of long-term debt for large corporations and because the accounting uses the effective interest method to amortize discount or premium over the bond's life. A bond issued below par (at a discount) implies the coupon rate is below the market rate; the discount must be amortized as additional interest expense over the bond's life so that the total interest expense matches the market rate applied to the book value.
key_concepts: Current liabilities: obligations due within one year; include accounts payable, accrued expenses, short-term notes, current portion of long-term debt, unearned revenue, taxes payable
Accounts payable: amounts owed to suppliers for goods or services received on credit; typically no interest if paid within terms; often with discounts for early payment
Notes payable: written promises to pay specific amounts at specific future dates; typically interest-bearing
Accrued liabilities: expenses recognized but not yet paid — wages, interest, taxes, utilities; recorded through adjusting entries at period end

Contingent liabilities: potential obligations that depend on future events; recognized only if probable and reasonably estimable; disclosed in footnotes if possible but not probable; lawsuit exposure is the most common example
Bonds payable: long-term debt instruments that promise to pay a face amount at maturity and periodic interest payments; typically have maturities of 5-30 years

Bond terminology: face value (principal at maturity), coupon rate (stated interest rate), market rate (rate demanded by investors at issuance), discount (below par because coupon < market), premium (above par because coupon > market)

Effective interest method: computes interest expense as the effective (market) rate times the book value of the bond at the beginning of the period; any difference between interest expense and cash interest paid is amortization

Straight-line amortization: alternative method that allocates discount or premium equally over the bond's life; simpler but distorts pattern; only allowed when difference from effective interest is immaterial
Debt-to-equity ratio: Total Liabilities divided by Total Equity; measures leverage; higher ratios indicate more financial risk; varies widely by industry
lecture_notes.demonstration: Issue a 5-year bond with $1,000,000 face, 6% annual coupon (semiannual), when market rate is 8%. Semiannual coupon = $30,000. Semiannual market rate = 4%. PV of coupons = $30,000 × [1 - (1.04)^-10] / 0.04 = $243,325. PV of face = $1,000,000 / (1.04)^10 = $675,564. Issue price = $918,889. Discount = $81,111. Issuance: debit Cash $918,889, debit Discount on Bonds Payable $81,111, credit Bonds Payable $1,000,000. Period 1: 4% × $918,889 = $36,756 interest expense; cash paid $30,000; amortization $6,756. Entry: debit Interest Expense $36,756, credit Cash $30,000, credit Discount on Bonds Payable $6,756. Total interest expense over 10 periods = total coupons + total discount = $300,000 + $81,111 = $381,111, equivalent to 8% effective rate.
setup_requirements: Time value of money calculator or Excel PV/FV functions

review.value: Analysts calculate working capital and quick ratios from current items; misclassifying a liability as long-term understates near-term obligations

review.key: Bond Prices Move Inversely to Rates
review.value: When market rates rise, existing bonds' market value falls; pure time value of money mechanics
The effective interest method produces a constant interest rate on the bond's book value, which is the economic reality; straight-line produces a constant dollar amount but a varying rate
Lawsuits, environmental claims, and product liabilities can exceed the balance sheet value of the company; read contingencies carefully in any 10-K

class_schedule.topic: Stockholders' Equity: Common Stock, Preferred Stock, and Dividends
learning_objectives: Account for the issuance of common and preferred stock
Record cash dividends, stock dividends, and stock splits
Distinguish between retained earnings and other components of stockholders' equity
Calculate and interpret earnings per share (basic and diluted)
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 11 - Corporations: Organization, Stock Transactions, and Dividends
Kieso, Weygandt & Warfield, Intermediate Accounting (17th ed.), Ch. 15-16 - Stockholders' Equity and Dilutive Securities
Buffett, Berkshire Hathaway Annual Letters on share repurchases

lecture_notes.introduction: Stockholders' equity is the ownership claim on the company after all liabilities have been satisfied. For corporations, this claim is divided among holders of various classes of stock — typically common stock and sometimes preferred stock. The accounting for equity looks simple but involves subtleties: par value vs additional paid-in capital, the distinction between contributed capital and earned capital (retained earnings), treasury stock (the company's own stock bought back), and the effect of stock dividends and splits. The session also covers earnings per share (EPS), which is one of the most-watched numbers in corporate finance. EPS is reported on the income statement and drives analyst valuations, management compensation targets, and often the stock price reaction to earnings announcements. Since the 1980s, share repurchases have exceeded dividends as the primary means of returning capital in the US; the tax treatment is more favorable and the flexibility is greater, though the incentives can distort management behavior when EPS targets drive buyback decisions.
key_concepts: Corporation: a legal entity separate from its owners; offers limited liability, perpetual existence, transferability of ownership, ability to raise capital from many investors
Common stock: the primary form of ownership; holders vote on major decisions, elect directors, and receive residual claim on assets after creditors and preferred stockholders
Preferred stock: a hybrid security with features of both debt and equity; typically pays a fixed dividend, has priority over common stock in dividends and liquidation, usually no voting rights
Par value: an arbitrary amount assigned to each share when the corporation is formed; mostly a legacy concept today; par value is usually nominal ($0.01 or $0.001)
Additional paid-in capital: the excess of the issue price over par value; represents the bulk of contributed capital for most corporations
Retained earnings: cumulative net income minus cumulative dividends; represents earned capital reinvested in the business
Treasury stock: the corporation's own stock repurchased; reduces total equity (contra-equity account); does not receive dividends or vote; can be reissued later
Cash dividends: direct cash payments to shareholders; declared by the board, with three key dates — declaration, record, payment; reduce both cash and retained earnings
Stock dividends: distribution of additional shares rather than cash; do not change total equity; redistribute among accounts
Stock splits: proportional increase in shares outstanding with proportional decrease in par value; no accounting entry required, just disclosure
Earnings per share (EPS): Net Income minus Preferred Dividends, divided by Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding; the most-watched ratio in equity analysis
Diluted EPS: assumes all dilutive potential shares (options, convertibles) are converted; always less than or equal to basic EPS; GAAP requires disclosure of both
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through a growing company's equity transactions. (1) IPO: issue 10M shares at $20, par $0.01 — debit Cash $200M, credit Common Stock $100,000, credit APIC $199.9M. (2) Net income of $25M increases retained earnings. (3) $5M cash dividends — debit Retained Earnings $5M, credit Cash $5M. (4) Repurchase 500K shares at $30 — debit Treasury Stock $15M, credit Cash $15M. (5) 2-for-1 stock split: shares double, par halves, no entry. (6) Year 3 EPS: net income $40M, weighted average shares 19M, basic EPS = $2.11. With 1M options at $15 strike, treasury stock method adds ~500K shares, diluted EPS = $2.05.
setup_requirements: Sample stockholders' equity statement (Apple or Microsoft 10-K)

review.value: Contributed capital (investors' money: common stock + APIC) and earned capital (business earnings: retained earnings); they behave differently and are tracked separately
Dividends are distributions of profit to owners; they reduce equity and cash but never appear on the income statement; this distinguishes them from interest
Since the 1980s, share repurchases have exceeded dividends as the primary means of returning capital; tax treatment is more favorable and flexibility is greater
The P/E ratio uses EPS; analyst models forecast EPS; management often manages to EPS targets; if EPS is not the right lens, you need to know why

learning_objectives: Distinguish among cash flows from operating, investing, and financing activities
Prepare a statement of cash flows using the indirect method
Reconcile net income to cash flow from operations
Interpret the statement of cash flows and identify common red flags
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 12 - Statement of Cash Flows
FASB ASC 230 - Statement of Cash Flows
Mulford & Comiskey, Creative Cash Flow Reporting (2005), Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: The statement of cash flows is the most important financial statement that students and many investors still struggle to read. It answers a deceptively simple question: where did cash come from and where did it go during the period? Because the income statement is prepared on the accrual basis, it does not tell you how much cash the business generated. A company can report strong net income while its cash balance is shrinking, often a sign that receivables are growing, inventory is piling up, or capital expenditures are exceeding operations. Warren Buffett famously focuses on cash flow from operations minus capital expenditures — what he calls 'owner earnings' — rather than on reported net income, precisely because cash flow is harder to manipulate. The statement is divided into three sections: operating, investing, and financing. The operating section can be prepared two ways — direct method or indirect method. Over 95% of US companies use the indirect method. The reconciliation from net income to cash flow from operations is the most important thing to understand in this course.
key_concepts: Statement of cash flows: reports cash inflows and outflows during a period, classified into operating, investing, and financing activities; required under GAAP and IFRS
Cash and cash equivalents: includes cash, demand deposits, and short-term highly liquid investments with original maturities of three months or less
Operating activities: cash effects of transactions that enter into the determination of net income — customer receipts, supplier payments, employee wages, interest received and paid, income taxes
Investing activities: purchases and sales of long-lived assets and investments — buying equipment, selling land, making loans, buying securities
Financing activities: transactions with creditors and owners — issuing stock, repurchasing stock, paying dividends, borrowing, repaying debt
Indirect method (operating section): start with net income; add back non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization); adjust for gains and losses on asset sales; adjust for changes in working capital
Working capital adjustments: an increase in a current asset (other than cash) uses cash; a decrease provides cash; an increase in a current liability provides cash; a decrease uses cash
Free cash flow: typically defined as cash flow from operations minus capital expenditures; measures cash available for discretionary uses (dividends, buybacks, debt reduction, acquisitions)

Red flags: net income growing while operating cash flow shrinks; large gap between net income and CFO; heavy reliance on financing to fund operations; consistently negative free cash flow despite profitable operations
Owner earnings (Buffett): operating cash flow minus the capital expenditures required to maintain competitive position; a stricter measure of sustainable free cash flow
lecture_notes.demonstration: Prepare a statement of cash flows. Given: Net Income $50,000; Depreciation $15,000; Loss on sale of equipment $3,000; AR increased $8,000; Inventory decreased $5,000; AP increased $4,000; Wages payable decreased $2,000; Purchased new equipment for $40,000; Sold old equipment for $7,000 (book value $10,000); Issued $20,000 of common stock; Paid $10,000 dividends; Repaid $5,000 of debt. OPERATING: $50,000 + $15,000 + $3,000 - $8,000 + $5,000 + $4,000 - $2,000 = $67,000. INVESTING: -$40,000 + $7,000 = -$33,000. FINANCING: $20,000 - $10,000 - $5,000 = $5,000. Net change in cash = $39,000. Free cash flow = $67,000 - $40,000 = $27,000.
setup_requirements: Sample cash flow statements (any consumer products 10-K)

review.value: Profitable companies can run out of cash; unprofitable companies can accumulate cash; the income statement and cash flow statement answer different questions, and you need both

review.key: Operating Section Is Where the Truth Is
review.value: Operating cash flow is harder to manipulate than net income; sustained divergence between CFO and net income is a warning sign
A big positive contribution to CFO from falling receivables is not sustainable; analysts normalize working capital changes to see underlying trends

review.key: Free Cash Flow Is What Shareholders Own
review.value: Free cash flow is the cash available to pay dividends, buy back shares, reduce debt, or acquire competitors; consistent positive FCF is one of the hallmarks of a high-quality business

class_schedule.topic: Financial Statement Analysis: Ratios and Trends
learning_objectives: Calculate and interpret liquidity ratios (current ratio, quick ratio, cash ratio)
Calculate and interpret solvency ratios (debt-to-equity, times interest earned)
Calculate and interpret profitability ratios (gross margin, net margin, ROA, ROE)
Apply horizontal and vertical analysis to identify trends and structural changes
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), Ch. 13 - Financial Analysis: The Big Picture
Penman, Financial Statement Analysis and Security Valuation (5th ed.), Ch. 7-9
Graham, The Intelligent Investor (rev. ed., 2006), Ch. 14

lecture_notes.introduction: The previous 13 sessions taught you how to produce financial statements. This session teaches you how to read them. Financial statement analysis is the set of techniques used to draw conclusions from financial data about a company's liquidity, solvency, profitability, and efficiency. Analysts use these techniques to value companies, make credit decisions, identify undervalued stocks, and spot problems before they become disasters. The techniques fall into three categories. Ratio analysis computes standardized metrics that allow comparison across firms. Horizontal analysis looks at trends over multiple periods. Vertical analysis expresses every income statement line as a percentage of sales and every balance sheet line as a percentage of total assets, revealing structural changes that dollar amounts can hide. No single technique is sufficient; the three are complementary. A high current ratio without context says nothing; the same ratio compared to industry peers and the company's own history is informative. DuPont analysis decomposes ROE into three drivers — net margin, asset turnover, and financial leverage — revealing which levers produce returns.
key_concepts: Liquidity ratios: Current Ratio (current assets / current liabilities; typical target 1.5-2), Quick Ratio (cash + receivables / current liabilities), Cash Ratio (cash / current liabilities)
Solvency ratios: Debt-to-Equity (total liabilities / total equity), Debt Ratio (total liabilities / total assets), Times Interest Earned (EBIT / interest expense)

Profitability ratios: Gross Margin (gross profit / sales), Operating Margin (operating income / sales), Net Margin (net income / sales), Return on Assets (net income / avg total assets), Return on Equity (net income / avg total equity)
Efficiency ratios: Inventory Turnover, Accounts Receivable Turnover, Asset Turnover (sales / avg total assets)
Market ratios: Price/Earnings (P/E), Price/Book, Dividend Yield, Earnings Yield
Horizontal analysis: compares line items across multiple periods; computes percentage change from base period; reveals growth and contraction trends
Vertical analysis: expresses each line item as a percentage of a base amount; enables comparison across companies of different sizes
DuPont analysis: decomposes ROE into three drivers — Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier; shows which levers produce returns
Benchmarking: compares a company's ratios to industry averages to identify relative strengths and weaknesses
Limitations of ratio analysis: accounting differences between firms, different fiscal year-ends, one-time items, industry-specific conventions, historical cost distortions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze Walmart vs Target. Walmart 2024: Revenue $648B, Net Income $16B, Assets $252B, Equity $83B. Target 2024: Revenue $107B, Net Income $4.1B, Assets $55B, Equity $11.2B. Gross Margin: Walmart 24%, Target 27%. Operating Margin: Walmart 4%, Target 6%. ROA: Walmart 6%, Target 7.5%. ROE: Walmart 19%, Target 37%. DuPont ROE — Walmart: 2.5% × 2.6 × 3.0 = 19%; Target: 3.8% × 1.9 × 4.9 = 37%. Target earns higher margin per sale (differentiated assortment) but Walmart turns assets over faster (efficient supply chain). Target uses more leverage. Target's higher ROE is real but comes with higher risk from leverage.
setup_requirements: Financial analysis spreadsheet with ratio formulas
Walmart and Target 10-K filings from SEC EDGAR
Industry benchmarks from Damodaran or Bloomberg

review.value: A 2.0 current ratio is neither good nor bad in isolation; compared to industry peers, the firm's history, and the business model, it becomes informative
Two companies with the same ROE can be very different; one earning 19% via high margins and the other via high leverage face very different risks
Common-size statements make it easy to see that SG&A is drifting up or gross margins are eroding; dollar amounts can hide these trends in a growing business
A ratio that looks bad is a question, not a conclusion; it prompts investigation into why, which is where real analysis begins

class_schedule.topic: Course Synthesis: Ethics, Judgment, and the Future of Financial Reporting
learning_objectives: Synthesize the full accounting cycle from transactions through analysis
Recognize the role of judgment in financial reporting and identify common ethical pitfalls
Evaluate the impact of emerging issues: AI-assisted audit, ESG reporting, digital assets
Apply financial statement analysis to a new company on the final exam
readings: Weygandt, Kimmel & Kieso, Financial Accounting (11th ed.), end-of-chapter cases
Schilit & Perler, Financial Shenanigans (4th ed., 2018), Ch. 12
IFRS Foundation, 'IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards' overview

lecture_notes.introduction: We have walked the full accounting cycle: transactions, journal entries, adjustments, closing, financial statements, analysis. The mechanics are important but they are not the whole point of the course. The point is that financial accounting is the system by which businesses communicate their economic reality to people who cannot see inside.
