Done well, it enables efficient capital allocation and constrains management's ability to misrepresent results. Done badly, it enables fraud. This final session pulls the threads together and looks forward. The ethical dimension deserves emphasis. Unlike many professional fields, accountants have explicit ethical responsibilities codified in professional standards. Every fraud we have studied — Enron, WorldCom, Wirecard, Luckin Coffee — involved accountants who knew something was wrong and did not say so. The reasons are the reasons Cressey identified: pressure, opportunity, rationalization. The lesson for students who will become managers is that you will face situations where reporting the truth has costs and misreporting has short-term benefits. Your career will be shaped by how you respond. We also look forward. AI is changing audit. ESG reporting is creating new disclosures around carbon, labor, and governance. Digital assets are forcing FASB to issue new guidance. The accounting profession of 2040 will look different from 2025, but the core logic will still apply.
key_concepts: Integration of the accounting cycle: every transaction flows through the same pipeline from analysis to disclosure; understanding it end-to-end lets you trace any number in any financial statement back to its origin

The role of judgment: GAAP leaves many choices to management — revenue timing, useful lives, reserve estimates, goodwill impairment; two honest companies can report different numbers for the same underlying reality
Earnings management: using accounting choices to smooth earnings or meet targets; legal if within GAAP, unethical when it misleads, criminal when it crosses into fraud
AICPA Code of Professional Conduct: requires integrity, objectivity, independence, due professional care, adherence to professional standards; the auditor's duty is to the public interest
Sarbanes-Oxley's enduring effects: CEO/CFO certifications, internal control audits, auditor independence rules, PCAOB oversight, criminal penalties for fraud
ESG reporting: environmental, social, and governance disclosures increasingly required by regulators (EU's CSRD, SEC's climate rules) and demanded by investors
Sustainability accounting: extension of accounting to non-financial measures — carbon emissions, water use, workforce metrics, supply chain practices
Digital asset accounting: FASB ASU 2023-08 now requires fair-value measurement of cryptocurrencies with gains and losses in earnings
Continuous auditing and real-time reporting: audit firms increasingly use data analytics on full datasets rather than samples
Lifelong learning: the conceptual foundations are stable but the rules change every year; CPAs have continuing education requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Present a mini-fraud case: a technology company reports strong net income but its accounts receivable have grown faster than sales for four consecutive quarters, operating cash flow has lagged net income, and it recently extended payment terms. The CFO attributes the receivables growth to 'strategic customer investments.' What signals would concern an analyst? Expected response: the gap between net income and operating cash flow is a classic warning sign; the lengthening of payment terms may be disguising channel stuffing; the aging of receivables should be examined closely; management's explanation sounds rehearsed. This is exactly the pattern that preceded Computer Associates in the 1990s. Close by asking each student: knowing what you now know, what question would you ask of any company before investing?

review.value: The mechanics are arithmetic, but the purpose is communication of economic reality to outside users; every choice is in service of or opposition to that purpose
GAAP cannot eliminate judgment, and any system that tried would be both inflexible and manipulable; the goal is to make judgments transparent so users can evaluate them
Twenty years ago financial reporting meant financial numbers only; today it includes climate, labor, and governance disclosures; accounting skills apply to all of it
The techniques are morally neutral; the accountants and managers who use them are not; the field depends on people willing to tell the truth when it costs something

questions.question: For each transaction, show the journal entry and state the effect on the accounting equation: (a) Owner invests $40,000 cash and $10,000 equipment; (b) Purchases $6,000 of inventory on credit, 2/10 n/30; (c) Sells $3,000 of inventory for $5,000 cash (perpetual); (d) Prepays $3,000 for six months of rent; (e) Owner withdraws $500 for personal use.
questions.answer: (a) Debit Cash $40,000, debit Equipment $10,000, credit Common Stock $50,000. Assets +$50,000; Equity +$50,000. (b) Debit Inventory $6,000, credit Accounts Payable $6,000. Assets +$6,000; Liabilities +$6,000. (c) Two entries: debit Cash $5,000, credit Sales Revenue $5,000; debit COGS $3,000, credit Inventory $3,000. Net: Assets +$2,000; Equity +$2,000. (d) Debit Prepaid Rent $3,000, credit Cash $3,000. Assets unchanged. (e) Debit Dividends $500, credit Cash $500. Assets -$500; Equity -$500.

rubric.key: Correct journal entries for all five transactions

questions.question: Explain why accrual accounting is required by GAAP for most businesses, using a concrete example where cash and accrual produce different results. Describe the GAAP principles being applied.
questions.answer: GAAP requires accrual because it produces a more accurate picture of economic performance than cash basis. Example: A construction company signs a $2M contract December 1 and receives $500K upfront. By December 31, 20% of work ($400K) is complete. Cash basis shows $500K revenue in December, overstating by $100K. Accrual shows $400K revenue, with $100K as unearned revenue (liability). Principles applied: (1) Revenue recognition — revenue recognized when performance obligations satisfied, not when cash received (ASC 606). (2) Matching principle — costs incurred to do the $400K work are recognized in the same period. (3) Periodicity — results allocated to correct reporting period.

rubric.key: Clear accrual vs cash explanation with concrete example

questions.question: A trial balance has Debit $487,500 and Credit $486,600 — a difference of $900. What are three possible causes, and how would you search for each?
questions.answer: (1) TRANSPOSITION ERROR: Digits reversed. $900 is divisible by 9, plausible. Search: look for amounts around $900, $1,800, $2,700 where digits might be swapped. (2) OMITTED POSTING: A credit entry was journalized but not posted. Search: compare journal to ledger for missing entries. (3) WRONG SIDE: A $450 credit posted as debit produces $900 difference. Search: look for $450 entries and verify posting side. (4) INCORRECT AMOUNT: $900 entry recorded as zero or $1,800. Search: scan recent entries for amounts matching $900.

rubric.key: Three plausible error causes matched to $900 difference

questions.question: A company's year-end unadjusted trial balance shows: Prepaid Insurance $6,000 (policy paid July 1 for 12 months); Supplies $1,500 ($400 on hand); Unearned Service Revenue $8,000 ($5,000 earned); Equipment $60,000 (5-year life, purchased April 1). Prepare all adjusting entries.
questions.answer: (1) INSURANCE: 6 months used. Entry: debit Insurance Expense $3,000, credit Prepaid Insurance $3,000. (2) SUPPLIES: $1,500 - $400 = $1,100 used. Entry: debit Supplies Expense $1,100, credit Supplies $1,100. (3) UNEARNED REVENUE: $5,000 earned. Entry: debit Unearned Service Revenue $5,000, credit Service Revenue $5,000. (4) DEPRECIATION: 9 months of $12,000 annual = $9,000. Entry: debit Depreciation Expense $9,000, credit Accumulated Depreciation $9,000.

questions.question: A merchandiser reports for March: beginning inventory $20,000; purchases $80,000; returns $3,000; freight-in $2,000; ending inventory $25,000; net sales $150,000; operating expenses $30,000. Calculate COGS, gross profit, gross profit percentage, and operating income. If industry average gross margin is 35%, evaluate.
questions.answer: COGS: $20,000 + ($80,000 - $3,000 + $2,000) - $25,000 = $74,000. Gross Profit: $150,000 - $74,000 = $76,000. Gross Profit % = 50.67%. Operating Income: $76,000 - $30,000 = $46,000. EVALUATION: 50.67% is significantly above industry 35%. Could indicate pricing power, lower product cost, or premium segment. Investigation needed: is this sustained? Compare prior years; examine product mix; understand competitive positioning. A 15-point premium is valuable if sustainable.

questions.question: Explain the purpose of closing entries and why revenue and expense accounts are closed while asset and liability accounts are not. Walk through the four closing entries for a service business with Revenue $100,000, Rent $12,000, Wages $60,000, Utilities $3,000, Dividends $5,000.
questions.answer: PURPOSE: Closing entries (1) zero out temporary accounts so the next period starts fresh, and (2) transfer net income to retained earnings. TEMPORARY VS PERMANENT: Revenue and expense accounts are TEMPORARY — they measure activity during a specific period. Asset and liability accounts are PERMANENT — they measure cumulative position and persist. FOUR CLOSING ENTRIES: (1) Close revenues: debit Service Revenue $100,000, credit Income Summary $100,000. (2) Close expenses: debit Income Summary $75,000, credit Rent $12,000, credit Wages $60,000, credit Utilities $3,000. (3) Close Income Summary to Retained Earnings: debit Income Summary $25,000, credit Retained Earnings $25,000. (4) Close Dividends: debit Retained Earnings $5,000, credit Dividends $5,000. After entries: Retained Earnings increased by $20,000, all temporary accounts zero.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of closing entry purpose
All four closing entries correctly prepared

questions.question: FIFO costing. Beginning inventory: 50 units @ $10 = $500. May 3: buy 100 @ $12 = $1,200. May 15: buy 80 @ $14 = $1,120. May 28: sell 180 units. Calculate COGS and ending inventory under (a) FIFO, (b) LIFO, (c) weighted average. Which method produces highest income, and why are tax considerations often the driver?
questions.answer: Total units: 230. Total cost: $2,820. Sold: 180. Ending: 50 units. (a) FIFO: COGS = 50×$10 + 100×$12 + 30×$14 = $500 + $1,200 + $420 = $2,120. Ending = 50×$14 = $700. (b) LIFO: COGS = 80×$14 + 100×$12 = $1,120 + $1,200 = $2,320. Ending = 50×$10 = $500. (c) WEIGHTED AVERAGE: $2,820/230 = $12.26/unit. COGS = 180×$12.26 = $2,206.96. Ending = 50×$12.26 = $613.04. HIGHEST INCOME: FIFO (lowest COGS $2,120) produces highest gross profit. TAX MOTIVATION: LIFO produces lowest taxable income during rising prices. IRS conformity rule requires LIFO financial reporting if used for tax. ExxonMobil's LIFO reserve of $7B represents cumulative tax savings.

questions.question: Aging: Current $200,000 (1%); 1-30 days $80,000 (3%); 31-60 $40,000 (10%); 61-90 $20,000 (25%); Over 90 $10,000 (50%). Allowance has credit balance $3,500. (a) Calculate required ending allowance. (b) Prepare adjusting entry. (c) Prepare write-off entry for $1,200 uncollectible.
questions.answer: (a) REQUIRED: $2,000 + $2,400 + $4,000 + $5,000 + $5,000 = $18,400. (b) ADJUSTING: Need to increase allowance from $3,500 to $18,400. Bad debt expense = $14,900. Entry: debit Bad Debt Expense $14,900, credit Allowance $14,900. (c) WRITE-OFF: debit Allowance $1,200, credit Accounts Receivable $1,200. No income statement impact because expense was already recognized. Allowance drops to $17,200.

rubric.key: Correct aging calculations and total allowance
Correct adjusting entry with delta calculation
Correct write-off with no income statement effect

questions.question: Explain the relationship between the fraud triangle and internal controls, using a concrete example of how segregation of duties prevents a specific fraud.
questions.answer: FRAUD TRIANGLE: Cressey — fraud requires pressure, opportunity, and rationalization. Only OPPORTUNITY can be directly controlled through internal controls. SEGREGATION EXAMPLE: Accounts payable fraud — without segregation, one employee could create fictitious vendors, approve invoices, and route payments. WITH SEGREGATION: (1) Person A sets up vendors with documentation verification; (2) Person B approves invoices against purchase orders; (3) Person C generates payments. Fictitious-vendor fraud now requires collusion of three people — much harder, easier to detect. Also catches honest errors. Real example: Koss Corporation 2009-2010 lost $30M+ to embezzlement by VP Finance with unchecked access; after discovery, rigorous segregation was implemented.

rubric.key: Correct identification that only opportunity is directly controllable
Concrete segregation example preventing specific fraud

questions.question: Machine purchased $80,000, freight $3,000, installation $2,000. Life 10 years, salvage $5,000. (a) Calculate capitalized cost and annual straight-line. (b) Year 1 depreciation using double-declining-balance. (c) Beginning of year 3, revise: remaining life 5 years, salvage $2,000. Calculate revised straight-line.
questions.answer: (a) Capitalized cost = $80,000 + $3,000 + $2,000 = $85,000. Straight-line = ($85,000 - $5,000)/10 = $8,000/year. (b) DDB: Rate = 2×(1/10) = 20%. Year 1 = 20% × $85,000 = $17,000. Book value end year 1 = $68,000. (c) REVISED (prospective): Book value at beginning of year 3 under straight-line = $85,000 - $16,000 = $69,000. Remaining depreciable = $69,000 - $2,000 = $67,000. Revised annual = $67,000/5 = $13,400/year. Apply prospectively years 3-7.

questions.question: Issue $500,000 face 10-year bonds, 4% annual coupon, market rate 6%. (a) Calculate issue price. (b) Journalize issuance. (c) First interest entry using effective interest. (d) Par, discount, or premium? Why?
questions.answer: (a) PV of face = $500,000/(1.06)^10 = $279,199. PV of coupons = $20,000 × 7.3601 = $147,202. Issue price ≈ $426,401. Discount = $73,599. (b) ISSUANCE: debit Cash $426,401, debit Discount on Bonds Payable $73,599, credit Bonds Payable $500,000. (c) FIRST INTEREST: Interest expense = 6% × $426,401 = $25,584. Cash = 4% × $500,000 = $20,000. Amortization = $5,584. Entry: debit Interest Expense $25,584, credit Discount on Bonds Payable $5,584, credit Cash $20,000. (d) DISCOUNT — coupon 4% below market 6%, so investors only pay less than face to earn the market return.

questions.question: Compare effects of (a) $10M cash dividend, (b) $10M stock dividend, (c) 2-for-1 stock split. State balance sheet changes, effect on total equity, and economic value.
questions.answer: (a) CASH DIVIDEND: Cash -$10M, Retained Earnings -$10M. Total equity -$10M. Real cash leaves company. (b) STOCK DIVIDEND (10% at par $10, market $50): Retained Earnings -$50M, Common Stock +$10M, APIC +$40M. Total equity unchanged — only composition changes. Cash unchanged. Economic value unchanged — each shareholder owns more shares of same total company. (c) 2-FOR-1 SPLIT: No journal entry. Shares double, par halves, total equity unchanged. Economic value ZERO effect. Tesla 3-for-1 (2022), Apple 4-for-1 (2020) are cosmetic for price range appeal. LESSON: Only cash dividends actually transfer value to shareholders.

rubric.key: Correct stock split with no entry

questions.question: Net Income $50M, Depreciation $20M, AR +$8M, Inventory -$5M, AP +$3M, Loss on sale $2M, Equipment purchase $30M, Equipment sale $5M (book value $7M), Bonds issued $25M, Dividends $12M. Prepare statement of cash flows using indirect method and calculate free cash flow.
questions.answer: OPERATING: $50 + $20 + $2 - $8 + $5 + $3 = $72M. INVESTING: -$30 + $5 = -$25M. FINANCING: $25 - $12 = $13M. Net change = $60M. FREE CASH FLOW = $72M - $30M = $42M. Dividends of $12M well covered; $30M remains for debt reduction or buybacks. FCF/Net Income = 0.84, healthy — operating cash flow and reported income broadly consistent.

rubric.key: Correct operating section with all adjustments

questions.question: Calculate ratios: Net Sales $600M, COGS $360M, Operating Expenses $180M, Net Income $45M, Total Assets $500M, Total Equity $200M, Current Assets $150M, Current Liabilities $100M, Inventory $80M, Total Debt $300M. Calculate current, quick, gross margin, net margin, ROA, ROE, debt/equity, asset turnover. Do DuPont decomposition.
questions.answer: Current ratio = $150/$100 = 1.5. Quick = ($150-$80)/$100 = 0.7. Gross margin = 40%. Net margin = 7.5%. ROA = $45/$500 = 9%. ROE = $45/$200 = 22.5%. Debt/equity = $300/$200 = 1.5. Asset turnover = $600/$500 = 1.2x. DUPONT: ROE = Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier = 7.5% × 1.2 × 2.5 = 22.5%. ✓ Equity multiplier = $500/$200 = 2.5. INTERPRETATION: Strong 22.5% ROE driven by moderate margin, adequate turnover, and significant leverage. The 2.5 equity multiplier contributes importantly — lower leverage would drop ROE. Risk comes with the leverage.

questions.question: A software company reports growing revenue and net income but falling operating cash flow. Deferred revenue is decreasing, AR growing faster than sales, and payment terms just extended to largest customers. Diagnose and list questions for management.
questions.answer: DIAGNOSIS: Earnings quality concerns. Specific signals: (1) Growing net income with FALLING operating cash flow signals earnings diverging from economic reality. (2) Decreasing deferred revenue — either growth is slowing or recognition accelerated. (3) AR growing FASTER than sales means selling without collecting. (4) Extended payment terms confirms management using concessions to close deals. QUESTIONS: (1) Receivables aging? What % is 60+ days past due? (2) Allowance as % of receivables? Has it changed? (3) What specific contractual changes to largest customers? Right to return? Delayed payment? (4) Why is deferred revenue decreasing? (5) CFO-net income reconciliation for 8 quarters? (6) Customer concentration? (7) DSO trend? (8) Audit committee concerns? REAL-WORLD: This pattern preceded Computer Associates (1990s) and Valeant Pharmaceuticals (2015-2016). Evasive answers are themselves a signal.

rubric.key: Clear diagnosis of divergence as key signal

major_assignments.title: Accounting Cycle Project: From Transaction to Trial Balance
major_assignments.description: Complete a simulated accounting cycle for a small service or merchandising business. You will receive 30-40 transactions for the first two months of operations. Record all transactions in a general journal, post to the general ledger, prepare an unadjusted trial balance, make adjusting entries, prepare an adjusted trial balance, prepare financial statements, and perform closing entries. The project integrates weeks 1-5.
objectives: Apply the full accounting cycle from transaction analysis through closing
Prepare journal entries for a variety of transaction types including adjustments
Produce a complete set of financial statements in proper format
Demonstrate understanding of the articulation among the financial statements
requirements: Complete general journal with all transactions and adjustments
Posting to general ledger T-accounts with running balances
Unadjusted trial balance, adjusted trial balance, and post-closing trial balance
Income statement, statement of retained earnings, and classified balance sheet
Closing entries fully journalized and posted
Written memo (1 page) discussing one judgment call you had to make
deliverables: Complete accounting cycle workbook (Excel or handwritten)
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on accuracy of entries, posting, adjustments, and financial statements. Small computational errors lose points proportionally; conceptual errors lose more.

rubric.key: Closing entries and post-closing trial balance

major_assignments.title: Financial Statement Analysis of a Public Company
major_assignments.description: Select a publicly traded company (not Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Tesla) and conduct a comprehensive financial statement analysis using the three most recent annual 10-K filings from SEC EDGAR. Produce the kind of write-up a buy-side or credit analyst would prepare.
objectives: Read and interpret a real-world 10-K filing
Compute and interpret liquidity, solvency, profitability, and efficiency ratios over three years
Conduct horizontal and vertical analysis of income statement and balance sheet
Identify concerns or strengths based on the analysis
requirements: Ratio analysis table: at least 12 ratios for three years
Common-size income statement and balance sheet for three years
Horizontal analysis showing YoY growth rates
Discussion of at least two 10-K notes affecting the analysis
deliverables: Supporting Excel file with all calculations
Copy of 10-K filings used (or SEC EDGAR links)
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on accuracy of ratio calculations, depth of interpretation (facts rather than generic statements), use of notes disclosures, and quality of analytical narrative.

rubric.key: Ratio calculations accurate for three years

major_assignments.title: Accounting Ethics Case Study
major_assignments.description: Read one of the assigned real-world accounting fraud cases (Enron, WorldCom, Wirecard, Luckin Coffee, Theranos, Satyam, Toshiba) from primary sources including SEC enforcement releases. Analyze the fraud using the fraud triangle and COSO framework, identify specific accounting shenanigans, and discuss consequences and lessons.
objectives: Apply the fraud triangle and COSO framework to a real fraud case
Evaluate the role of the external auditor
Draw lessons for current and future financial reporting practice
requirements: At least 5 primary sources (SEC releases, court filings, investigative journalism)
Fraud triangle analysis with case-specific pressure, opportunity, rationalization
COSO framework analysis identifying which control components failed
Lessons with at least two specific recommendations
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on depth of engagement, correct framework application, quality of auditor analysis, and thoughtfulness of lessons.

rubric.key: Fraud triangle analysis specific to case

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 6, 9, 12, 15) covering transactions, adjustments, closing, merchandising, inventory, internal control, receivables, long-lived assets, liabilities, equity, cash flows, and analysis

class_participation.description: Active participation in demonstrations, problem walkthroughs, and case discussions

major_assignments.description: Three major assignments: accounting cycle project (100 pts), financial statement analysis (100 pts), accounting ethics case (75 pts)

midterm_examination.description: Examination covering weeks 1-7: foundations, accounting cycle, accrual accounting, merchandising, inventory costing

final_examination.description: Comprehensive examination emphasizing second-half content: long-lived assets, liabilities, equity, cash flows, analysis, ethics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected at every session because problem walkthroughs are cumulative. Two unexcused absences allowed; further absences reduce participation grade. Students are responsible for missed material.
course_policies.late_submissions: Quizzes must be taken during the scheduled time; makeups require documented excuse. Major assignments are due at the start of class on the due date; late penalty 10% per calendar day for up to three days, then zero.
course_policies.collaboration: Quiz answers must be individual. The accounting cycle project is individual. The financial statement analysis may be discussed with classmates but written analysis must be individual. The ethics case is individual. Unauthorized collaboration is plagiarism.
course_policies.academic_integrity: AI for accounting calculations is prohibited in quizzes and examinations. AI as a research aid on major assignments (explaining a ratio, looking up a rule) is permitted but must be disclosed. Substantially AI-generated work is plagiarism. All sources — textbook, SEC filings, news articles, AI tools — must be cited.
